comments.text: This problem has a substantial literature. The most impressive theoretical breakthrough on this problem is probably the work of Bennett and Siksek, who proved there are no solutions to the equation $$n(n+d)(n+2d) \cdots (n+(k-1)d)=y^\ell$$ in integers $n,d,k,y,\ell$ with $\text{gcd}(n,d)=1$, $d\geq 1$, $ny \neq 0$, $k$ large, and $\ell > \exp(10^k)$ a prime. The bibliography of their paper will lead one to many other works. As other comments have noted, this Diophantine equation has solutions if we relax the condition that $n,n+d,\ldots$ are all positive. From the Bennett-Siksek paper and some other works, one can extract the conjecture that all solutions to this equation in integers $n,d,y$ with $\text{gcd}(n,d)=1, y \neq 0, d\geq 1, k \geq 4,\ell \geq 2$ come from one of the following situations: --$(k,\ell,n,d)=(4,3,-9,5)$ or $(4,3,-6,5)$ --$k \equiv 0 \pmod{4}$ and $(\ell,n,d)=(2,-k+1,2)$, or --$k$ is an odd square and $(\ell,n,d)=(2,-k+2,2)$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
FWIW, Győry–Hajdu–Pintér show this is impossible for $4\leq k\leq 34$ here. This was located by ChatGPT. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

An even easier example in the case of negative integers being allowed is $(-3)×(-1)×1×3=3^2$ and in general any product of an even length partial sequence of odd numbers starting with one, together with their symmetrics.
It was proved in 2006 that the length of the arithmetic progression must be at least 12, if the product is to be a perfect power. See here. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
